question: Explain the Write Allocation policy on a cache miss and state when it is beneficial.
expectedPoints: Write Allocation fetches the block from main memory into cache before performing the write operation
Also called Fetch-on-Write
Beneficial when there is spatial locality - subsequent reads/writes to nearby addresses will hit in cache
Reduces memory traffic for programs with write locality

question: What is No-Write Allocate policy? How does it differ from Write Allocation?
expectedPoints: No-Write Allocate writes directly to main memory without loading the block into cache
Does not change cache state on a write miss
Differs from Write Allocation which loads the block into cache first
Simpler to implement but may miss opportunities for caching written data

question: Explain the relationship between write-through caches and no-write allocate policy.
expectedPoints: Write-through caches write to both cache and main memory simultaneously
When combined with no-write allocate, write misses bypass the cache entirely
This combination avoids the overhead of loading blocks on write misses
Common choice for write-through caches as data is immediately propagated to memory

question: What is a cache line fill operation? When does it occur?
expectedPoints: Cache line fill transfers a block from main memory to an empty cache line
Occurs on a cache miss when the requested block is not in cache
Updates the tag, valid bit, and if applicable, the dirty bit
Necessary before the CPU can access the requested data

question: Explain write-back caching and its interaction with write allocation policy.
expectedPoints: Write-back delays writing to main memory until the cache line is evicted
Combined with write-allocate, the block is loaded into cache on write miss
The dirty bit tracks whether the line has been modified
Reduces memory traffic significantly compared to write-through

question: What factors determine the choice between write allocate and no-write allocate policies?
expectedPoints: Memory access patterns and presence of temporal/spatial locality
Write-to-read ratio of the application
Cache size and complexity constraints
Performance requirements and latency tolerance

question: Describe the role of eviction policy when allocating on a cache miss.
expectedPoints: Eviction policy is needed when cache is full on a miss
Determines which existing cache line to replace
Common policies include LRU, FIFO, and Random
The victim line must be written back to memory if dirty before loading new block

question: Compare and contrast Write-Through with No-Write Allocate versus Write-Back with Write-Allocate. Discuss the advantages, disadvantages, and typical use cases for each combination.
expectedPoints: WT+NWA: Simpler, no coherency issues, writes go directly to memory, higher memory traffic, good for simple systems
WB+WA: Complex, reduces memory traffic significantly, needs dirty bit management, better for applications with locality
WT+NWA advantages: Simplicity, data consistency, easier to implement
WB+WA advantages: Performance, reduced bus traffic, better for workloads with locality
Typical use cases: WT+NWA for embedded systems, simple processors; WB+WA for general-purpose processors and applications with good locality

question: Explain the process that occurs when a write miss happens in a write-back cache using write allocation policy. Include the state changes of relevant bits and memory operations.
expectedPoints: CPU generates write request for address not in cache
Cache controller detects miss and initiates fetch from main memory
Block is loaded into an available cache line (or evicts existing line)
Write operation modifies the cache line data
Dirty bit is set to indicate the line is modified
Modified data will be written to main memory only on eviction
This delay reduces memory traffic significantly

question: Design a decision algorithm for choosing between Write Allocation and No-Write Allocation policies. What characteristics of the workload would influence this choice?
expectedPoints: Consider spatial locality: if writes cluster in memory regions, WA is beneficial
Consider temporal locality: if written data is read soon after, WA helps
Consider working set size: if larger than cache, NWA may reduce cache pollution
Consider write intensity: high write volume with poor locality favors NWA
Consider data reuse patterns: streaming writes favor NWA, random writes favor WA
Hardware complexity: NWA is simpler to implement
